narrative:
Your law school application was denied because our admission model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 40.9% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use to ensure we select students poised for success. This prediction drew heavily from your academic record, LSAT score, family background, and planned full-time enrollment, compared against patterns from thousands of past students. The three biggest factors shaping this outcome were your LSAT performance, your Year 3 class ranking, and your Year 1 class ranking, in that order of influence.

Your LSAT score stood out as the strongest positive signal, well above the average for students who passed the bar, confidently pushing the model's view toward expecting you to succeed rather than fail. However, your Year 3 decile ranking of 2 was a notable drag, lower than the typical 5.91 average among bar passers, nudging the prediction toward higher failure risk by signaling challenges in sustaining strong performance later on. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1 pulled in the same concerning direction, far below the 6.04 average for successful students, highlighting early academic hurdles that the model weighed heavily.

Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 was slightly below the 3.24 average for bar passers, and while your upper-middle family income aligns with many who succeed, it didn't outweigh the ranking concerns. Planning full-time study matches the norm for 93% of bar passers, which was neutral here. Overall, your profile showed promise in test-taking strength but raised flags on consistent academic decile placement compared to those typically admitted, who cluster higher in school rankings.

To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT if possible to solidify that strength, but focus most on boosting your grades to climb those deciles—perhaps through targeted tutoring or lighter course loads initially. Reach out to academic advisors for personalized strategies proven to help students in similar positions pass the bar. Many have turned around early setbacks like yours; with deliberate steps, you can strengthen your profile for the next application cycle.
